coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots and two-dimensional porphyrinic MOFs for on-site monitoring of sulfide
coredata.dc:creator: Chai, Huining
Ma, Yuxin
Yuan, Zhishuang
Li, Yujie
Liu, Guoli
Chen, Le
Tian, Yong
Tan, Weiqiang
Ma, Jiping
Zhang, Guangyao
coredata.dc:description: Sulfide is a widespread environmental contaminant present in nature and can cause damage to human beings even in microscale amounts. Despite the success of existing point-of-care testing (POCT) techniques, on-site accurate and sensitive detection of sulfide remains a challenge. Herein, we proposed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots (CDs) and two-dimensional porphyrin MOFs. Further, a sensitive, economical and visual potable device with smartphone was developed for the real-time and accurate detection of sulfide. Using Color Identifier App, the fluorescent image information captured by the smartphone was converted to color intensity (RGB values), with limit of detection (LOD) of 6.75 μM for sulfide. This portable smartphone-based sensor was successfully used for the highly sensitive detection of sulfide in lake and tap water samples. The advantages of simple operation, low cost, and good selectivity make the sensing platform suitable for on-site sulfide monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfide
Carbon dots
Two-dimensional porphyrin MOFs
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Smartphone
Portable sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent test paper and detection of sulfide
Interaction between fluorescent probes and sulfide
Smartphone-based fluorescence sensor for sulfide detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22204089, 21801158), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB092), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M691689), the Shandong Province Higher Educational Program for Introduction and Cultivation of Young Innovative Talents (2021), State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University, Nos. ZKT23, KF2020201, GZRC202025). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis and the Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao University for sample tests.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22204089, 21801158), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB092), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M691689), the Shandong Province Higher Educational Program for Introduction and Cultivation of Young Innovative Talents (2021), State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University, Nos. ZKT23, KF2020201, GZRC202025). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis and the Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao University for sample tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (C) SEM, (D) TEM and (E) XRD of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn). (F) FT-IR spectra of TCPP, TCPP(Zn) and 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn).

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of (A) CDs, (B) 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) before and after the addition of different concentrations of sulfide. (C) UV–vis absorption and (D) fluorescence spectra of ratio fluorescent probes before and after the addition of sulfide; Inset: corresponding photographs under an UV lamp. (E) The high-resolution XPS response of Cu 2p spectra of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) before and after incubation with sulfide. (F) S 2p spectrum of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) after incubation with sulfide.

Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the ratio fluorescent probe (F 605/F 445) and (A) the HEPES buffer, (B) 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) dosage, (C) incubation time. The relationship between the color change (R/B) of the ratio fluorescent probe solution and (D) pH, (E) 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) dosage, (F) incubation time, respectively. Inset: corresponding photographs under an UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric fluorescent probe with different concentrations of sulfide (a–t: 0–400 μM) in 0.1 M, pH 7.4 HEPES buffer solution. (B) The calibration plot between F 605/F 445 and sulfide concentration. The calibration plots between R/B value and sulfide concentration: (C) the ratiometric fluorescent probe and (D) fluorometric test strips. Inset: corresponding photographs under an UV lamp.
Illustration of ratiometric fluorescent probe portable sensor for sulfide detection based on CDs and 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) MOFs.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots and two-dimensional porphyrinic MOFs for on-site monitoring of sulfide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao 266520, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Municipal Engineering, Qingdao University of Technology, Qingdao 266052, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology, College of Textiles and Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology
State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Intelligent and Wearable Technology, College of Textiles and Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfide is a widespread environmental contaminant present in nature and can cause damage to human beings even in microscale amounts. Despite the success of existing point-of-care testing (POCT) techniques, on-site accurate and sensitive detection of sulfide remains a challenge. Herein, we proposed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots (CDs) and two-dimensional porphyrin MOFs. Further, a sensitive, economical and visual potable device with smartphone was developed for the real-time and accurate detection of sulfide. Using Color Identifier App, the fluorescent image information captured by the smartphone was converted to color intensity (RGB values), with limit of detection (LOD) of 6.75 μM for sulfide. This portable smartphone-based sensor was successfully used for the highly sensitive detection of sulfide in lake and tap water samples. The advantages of simple operation, low cost, and good selectivity make the sensing platform suitable for on-site sulfide monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfides such as sodium sulfide and hydrogen sulfide are commonly found in the environment, originating mainly from industrial processes, and extremely harmful to environment and human' health [1]. In Addition, sulfides are often added as preservatives in food products due to their antioxidant and bactericidal properties [2] . Even at low concentrations, sulfide can cause irritation to the human conjunctiva, respiratory system and central system [3–6]. The standard for drinking water quality of China (GB5749-2022) stipulates that the sulfide content should not exceed 0.02 mg/L. Therefore, the accurate, rapid and efficient detection of sulfide in the environment is of great significance. Currently, the main detection methods for sulfide are colorimetry [7], chromatography [8], electrochemical assay [9], and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) [10]. All of these methods have achieved good results in sulfide detection, but they require huge and expensive instruments, well-trained researchers, complicated steps and high detection costs, which restrain their applications for point-of-care testing (POCT). Thus, the design and establishment of portable detection devices with rapid response, high stability and low cost has become a major focus in the field of industry and academia.

Currently, smartphone-based POCT has been widely used in health care [11–15], environmental monitoring [16–20], and food safety [21–25] due to the advantages of their easy-operation, low-cost, high efficiency, and ideal portability. Fluorescence technique has the merits of high sensitivity, excellent accuracy, and easy visualization, receiving increased attention in recent years for on-site testing [26–29]. However, most fluorescent probes are based on a single signal [30,31], which is susceptible to environmental interference and affects the accuracy of detection. In contrast, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing contains two different emission wavelengths, which can both improve detection sensitivity and accuracy by eliminating systematic errors through built-in corrections [14,32] and provide rich color variations that are easier to distinguish and visualize in real-time [33,34]. For example, Jiang et al. [35] prepared fluorescent test strips using ratiometric fluorescent probes and combined them with color recognition software of smartphone to construct a rapid analysis platform. Based on the linear relationship between the fluorescent color Red, Green and Blue (RGB) values of the paper strips and the Cd2+ concentration, a sensitive quantitative detection of Cd ions in rice samples was performed. In addition, Liu et al. [36] used a smartphone imaging sensor platform to read the RGB values of ratiometric fluorescent test strips to achieve highly sensitive on-site detection of fluoride ions. Zhang et al. [37] designed a detection device integrating a smartphone and ratiometric fluorescent test strips, which can achieve visual detection of phosphate in environmental water samples. However, there are fewer reports on the portable detection of sulfide by integrating smartphones and ratiometric fluorescence. Among them, the design of sensitive materials is the key to the superior performance of such sensors. Porphyrinic metal–organic frameworks (PMOFs) not only have the biochemical stability of porphyrin compounds, but also possess the large specific surface area and rich porosity of MOFs [38], and fluorescent sensors based on PMOFs have been widely used to detect environmental pollutants [39,40]. Carbon quantum dots, also known as carbon dots (CDs), are environmentally friendly nanomaterials with the advantages of simple synthesis, good stability, high water solubility, and low toxicity, and are extensively applied in biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, and photocatalysts [41,42]. Thus, the assembly of CDs and PMOFs is a promising method to construct portable fluorescent sensing materials.

In this study, CDs and two-dimensional copper-based zinc porphyrin MOF nanosheets (2D Cu-TCPP(Zn)) were prepared by hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, respectively. Then the two were mixed to prepare a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe and further designed a portable smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for on-site visualization and quantitative detection of sulfide in water (Scheme 1 ). The primary mechanism is that 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) does not have fluorescence due to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) effect. When sulfide is present in the system, the Cu node on 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) easily binds to the S2−, then the MOF structure disintegrates and the TCPP(Zn) molecules release, resulting in red fluorescence. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of CDs is not affected by sulfide. As the sulfide concentration increases, the detection system shows a clear color change process, from blue to purple and finally to pink, under UV light irradiation. The fluorescence images of the samples were collected by smartphone, and the color information was converted into color intensity (RGB values) by the Color Identifier App. The visualized quantitative detection of sulfide was realized based on the relationship between grayscale values and sulfide concentrations. The portable fluorescence sensing platform provides a new idea for the detection of sulfide in the environment.

Cu(NO3)2·3H2O was obtained from Adamas (China). meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphine (TCPP) was obtained from J&K Chemical ltd (Shanghai, China). Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. ltd (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was prepared with a Milli-Q purification system.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra were detected using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (T2602, Shanghai YoKe Instrument Co., China) and the fluorescence of all sample were detected by F97 (Shanghai Lengguang Technology, China) luminescence spectrometer. Morphology and size of the prepared MOF sheets was characterized by TALOS F200 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, USA) and field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (SU8100, HITACHI, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out with a Frontier NIR Std FT-IR Spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired using the Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250xi spectrometer. X–ray diffraction (XRD, Smartlab SE, Japan) data were collected in the 2θ range of 5–35° at a step size of 0.01°.

The CDs were prepared according to the previously reported method [43]. Specifically, 0.46 g of citric acid and 0.21 g of o-phenylenediamine were added sequentially to 10 mL of deionized water and dissolved by sonication for 10 min, then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined reactor and heated in an oven at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, a light yellow solution was obtained and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. The supernatant was put into a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the residue. Finally, the obtained CDs solution was lyophilized to obtain CDs yellow powder.

2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) was synthesized according to the reported literature with some modifications [44]. Solution A was obtained by weighing TCPP(Zn) (4.5 mg) and dissolving it in 3 mL DMF and 1 mL ethanol; solution B was obtained by sequentially adding Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (3.6 mg), PVP (10 mg), CF3COOH (10 μL), DMF (9 mL), and ethanol (3 mL) to a 50 mL round bottom flask. The reaction product was collected by centrifugation and washed by centrifugation with ethanol and ultrapure water alternately for three times, and dried to obtain 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn).

Sodium sulfide (Na2S) was used as the standard of sulfide ions. In a 5 mL centrifuge tube, 3 mL of HEPES buffer solution with pH = 7.4, 10 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL), 175 μL of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) (1 mg/mL), and 30 μL of sulfide at different concentrations were added sequentially, mixed and shaken for 10 min, and the fluorescence spectra were tested to plot the linear relationship between the intensity of fluorescence emission peak at 605 nm and the concentration of sulfide.

For the real sample testing, the sulfide concentrations in lake water and tap water samples were measured by the same method described above. The lake water samples were taken from He Yuan Lake in the eastern part of Qingdao University of Technology (QUT), and the tap water was taken from Laboratory 709 of QUT. All samples were pretreated by filtration with 0.22 mm microfiltration membrane before testing.

As shown in Fig. S2, the portable sensing device consists of the following parts: a dark box, a UV lamp, a cuvette, and a smartphone (Vivo X27 pro), which are combined to build a smartphone sensing platform. The size of the dark box of the smartphone sensing platform is 40 × 40 × 40 cm3, the UV lamp is placed at the left end of the dark box to provide light, the cuvette is placed at the center of the platform at the designated position, and the smartphone is placed at the designated cell phone placement area of the dark box to obtain fluorescent images. The RGB was read using the “Color Identifier” and a linear relationship between R/B values and sulfide concentration was plotted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent test paper and detection of sulfide
ce:section.ce:para: The filter paper was first cut into 1 × 3 cm2 strips, and then the strips were immersed in a mixture of CDs, 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) and different concentrations of sulfides. Among them, samples without sulfide were used to prepare blank test papers, dried at room temperature and repeated several times to enhance the fluorescence intensity. Their images were recorded under UV light using the above homemade portable device.

As shown in Fig. 1 A, the high-resolution TEM image showed that the CDs were spherical with good dispersion in the size range of 5.0–10 nm and distinct lattice stripes could be observed with a lattice spacing of 0.8 nm. The FT-IR spectrum was utilized to characterize the functional groups contained in the CDs (Fig. 1B). The characteristic absorption peaks at 3237, 3408, and 2930 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH, OH, and CH, respectively. 1707 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption peak of the stretching vibration of CO and the absorption peaks at 1577 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 belong to the bending vibrations of NH and CN, respectively. The CO stretching vibration peak locates at 1221 cm−1 [45]. The above results indicate the presence of hydrophilic groups such as OH, COOH and NH2 on the surface of the prepared CDs.

The SEM results displayed that the synthesized 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) nanosheet exhibited wrinkles and folds (Fig. 1C). Moreover, the TEM shows that the 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) has an ultrathin sheet-like structure (Fig. 1D). The crystal structure of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) was verified by XRD as shown in Fig. 1E. 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) has distinctive diffraction peaks at 5.8°, 7.5°, 12.5°, 19.8°, 22.5° and 30.9°, corresponding to (100), (110), (210), (320), (330) and (440) crystal planes of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn), which is consistent with those reported in the literature [46,47]. Additionally, the chemical functional groups of TCPP, TCPP(Zn) and 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) were compared by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 1F, the characteristic peak at 1691 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl group in TCPP and TCPP(Zn), which disappears in the FT-IR spectrum of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn), indicating the formation of the Cu2(COO)4 structural unit. Meanwhile, the disappearance of the characteristic NH peaks at 964 cm−1 and 3315 cm−1 means the presence of metal coordination in the porphyrin center of the 2D MOF structure. These results demonstrate the successful synthesis of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) nanosheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between fluorescent probes and sulfide
ce:section.ce:para: The interactions between CDs, 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) and ratiometric fluorescent probes and sulfide were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 , the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs were basically unchanged with or without the sulfide, and the aqueous solutions of CDs exhibited the characteristic blue luminescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 2A). Before the addition of sulfide, the 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) showed no significant UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission (Fig. 2B, black line). And, for the ratiometric fluorescent probes, the absorption peaks and fluorescence emission assigned to the 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) were not obvious except for the UV–vis absorption and blue fluorescence emission of CDs (Fig. 2C, D, black line). This was due to the LMCT effect between luminescent ligand ZnTCPP and Cu nodes in the MOF structure [44]. After the addition of sulfide, we could clearly observe the characteristic absorption peaks at 425 nm were both significantly enhanced, and the fluorescence emission peaks at 605 and 655 nm were also markedly enhanced (Fig. 2B, C, D). Meanwhile, the former only demonstrated red luminescence of porphyrins, while the luminescence color of the latter changed from blue to red under UV light irradiation, which was attributed to the easy binding of the Cu node on 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) to the S2−, disintegrating the MOF structure and releasing the TCPP(Zn). These experimental results indicate that the blue luminescence of CDs is not affected by sulfide, while 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) can interact with sulfide to produce red fluorescence from the otherwise non-luminescent MOFs. Accordingly, the ratiometric fluorescence signal is formed by the enhanced red emission generated by the interaction between 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) and sulfide with the blue emission of CDs as a reference, which enables the ratiometric and visual detection of sulfide.

Additionally, the high-resolution XPS response of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) was recorded to identify the elemental valence of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) before and after incubation with sulfide (Fig. 2E). The Cu 2p spectrum exhibited three main peaks at 932.9, 934.7 and 954.6 eV, and a satellite peak at 942.2 eV. After interaction with sulfide, the main peak at 934.7 eV and the satellite peak disappear, ascribed to the coordination of S and Cu. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 2F, the S 2p spectrum of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn)/S with two obvious peaks at 162.7 and 168.3 eV also proves that S was embedded in 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn), resulting in the generation of CuS [48]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra before and after binding of the 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) to the sulfide exhibited that the disappeared peak at 1691 cm−1 originating from the CO stretching vibration of carboxyl porphyrin reappear (Fig. S3), indicating that porphyrin molecules were dissociated from the framework structure. Therefore, the mechanism of sulfide detection is that, firstly, the blue fluorescence of CD is not affected by sulfide, while the Cu nodes on 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) can easily bind to S2−, resulting in the disintegration of the MOF structure and the release of TCPP(Zn) molecules, thus producing red fluorescence.

In order to achieve excellent fluorescence performance for the sensitive sulfide detection, the effects of pH of the buffer solution, the amount of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) and the incubation time were optimized by fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the Δ(F 605 /F 445) values increased with increasing pH in the range of 5.4 to 7.4, and reached the maximum at pH 7.4. The Δ(F 605 /F 445) value decreased when the pH is greater than 7.4, resulting from the structural instability of MOFs in alkaline environment and the dissociation of some porphyrin molecules from the framework and leading to a stronger background noise. Therefore, pH 7.4 was determined as the optimal condition for the detection system. Then, we fixed the concentration of CDs and explored the optimal concentration of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) (Fig. 3B). The F 605 /F 445 value gradually increased with the increase of 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) concentration, and reached the maximum value when 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) concentration was 58.3 μg/mL. As the concentration continued to increase, the F 605 /F 445 values remained basically unchanged (Fig. 3B). We then optimized the incubation time and the F 605 /F 445 value reached a maximum at an incubation time of 10 min, possessing a fast time response to sulfide (Fig. 3C).

Moreover, the RGB values were obtained using the smartphone “Color Identifier” App, and RGB ratio (R/B) was used as an indicator to further optimize the pH, 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) concentration and incubation time of the portable ratiometric fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. 3D–F, the response curves of this portable sensor for these three parameters are in general agreement with the results obtained from the above fluorescence spectra experiments.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe at 605 nm increased continuously with the increase of sulfide concentration. And, the fluorescence peak intensity at 445 nm fluctuates slightly under different sulfide concentration environments. This may be due to the random interaction (electrostatic or π-π conjugated interaction) of the porphyrin molecules dissociated from the MOF structure with CDs, resulting in the fluctuation of fluorescence emission [49,50]. The F 605 /F 445 values displayed a good linear relationship with the sulfides concentration in the range of 10–180 μM with a linear correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9952, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2.68 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 (Fig. 4B).

Meanwhile, the selectivity and anti-jamming stability of sulfide detection was investigated. Various potentially interfering anions (I−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, PO4 3−) did not affect the R/B values of the detection system. While for sulfides addition, R/B value significantly enhanced and the color changed distinctly, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescent probe has good selectivity and anti-jamming ability for sulfide (Fig. S6A). The ratiometric fluorescent probe was dispersed in HEPES buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4), stored at room temperature for 30 days and tested every 5 days, and its R/B value response to 100 μM sulfide showed little change (Fig. S6B), suggesting its high stability. Additionally, we investigated the reproducibility of the sensor. Five sets of ratiometric fluorescent probes prepared under the same conditions were placed in HEPES buffer containing 100 μM sulfide, and their R/B values were compared. A coefficient of variation of 2.26 % was obtained, indicating that the probe possesses excellent reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based fluorescence sensor for sulfide detection
ce:section.ce:para: Meanwhile, we established the standard equation based on the R/B value and sulfide concentration. In Fig. S5 and 4C, the R/B values for 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) and ratiometric fluorescence probes exhibited good linear relationships with sulfide concentrations in the ranges of 80–300 μM and 10–150 μM with LODs of 64.4 μM and 6.75 μM, respectively. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence probe displayed more obvious color changes under UV irradiation. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence probe has better performance for the detection of sulfide in water. We further designed the analytical platform of ratiometric fluorescence paper strips combined with smartphone for on-site visualization and quantitative detection of sulfides in water. As shown in Fig. 4D, the R/B value exhibited a good linear relationship with the sulfide concentration in the range of 0.2–0.7 mM with the LOD of 86.9 μM. These analytical performances were comparable to those obtained in some previous reports (Table S1). Additionally, the proposed smartphone-based fluorescence sensor has good selectivity, anti-jamming ability and high stability for sulfide (Fig. S7).

The response of the ratiometric fluorescence probe to sulfide in lake and tap water samples was tested by fluorescence spectrometry. The results are shown in Table S2, and the recoveries are all in the range of 92 % to 109 % with the relative standard deviation (RSD) values below 4.5 %. Hence, the proposed ratiometric fluorescence probe can be applied to the detection of sulfide in real environmental samples. Meanwhile, we also evaluated the feasibility of smartphone portable sensor for detecting sulfide in real samples. As shown in Table S3, the spiked recoveries ranged from 95 % to 110 % with RSD values less than 5.0 %. Then, we also investigated the fluorescent test strip for the detection of sulfide in lake water and tap water. As can be seen from Table S3, the spiked recoveries ranged from 98 % to 116 % with RSD values less than 5.0 %. These results suggest the developed portable ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be a promising candidate in practice for the detection of sulfide in the environment.

In summary, we successfully constructed a portable ratiometric fluorescence visualization sensor for highly sensitive on-site detection of sulfide. The blue light emission of CDs is not affected, while the Cu node on 2D Cu-TCPP(Zn) readily coordinates with S2−, resulting in the enhanced red light emission to form a ratiometric fluorescence signal. Under UV light irradiation, the ratiometric fluorescent probe showed a clear color change as the sulfide concentration increased. Fluorescence images of the solution or test strips were acquired by smartphone to obtain RGB values, and there was a good linear relationship between R/B values and sulfide concentrations. The ratiometric fluorescent probe displays high sensitivity, good stability and selectivity for sulfide. In addition, the portable sensor can be utilized in the detection of sulfide in real samples, providing a convenient and reliable method for environmental POCT.

Huining Chai: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Yuxin Ma: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhishuang Yuan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yujie Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Guoli Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Le Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yong Tian: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Weiqiang Tan: Supervision. Jiping Ma: Supervision. Guangyao Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22204089, 21801158), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB092), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M691689), the Shandong Province Higher Educational Program for Introduction and Cultivation of Young Innovative Talents (2021), State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University, Nos. ZKT23, KF2020201, GZRC202025). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis and the Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao University for sample tests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108288.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Relationship between functional bacteria in a denitrification desulfurization system under autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Huang, Q. Liu, Z.L. Li, X.D. Ma, Y.N. Hou, N.Q. Ren, A.J. Wang, Relationship between functional bacteria in a denitrification desulfurization system under autotrophic, heterotrophic, and mixotrophic conditions, Water Res. 188 (2021) 116526.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel selective detection method for sulfide in food systems based on the GMP-Cu nanozyme with laccase activity

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct Involvement of the acetato ligand in the reductive elimination step of rhodium-catalyzed methanol carbonylation
